title: Custom hooks for reusable logic

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific React: Component-Based Browser Applications draft for Custom hooks for reusable logic; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: React Learn and Reference; React 19.2; provisions applicable to Custom hooks for reusable logic
sources.claim: A custom hook packages reusable stateful logic and other hooks while each caller receives independent state unless an external store is shared.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A custom hook packages reusable stateful logic and other hooks while each caller receives independent state unless an external store is shared.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: useOnlineStatus owns one subscription pattern and cleans it up for each consuming component. Your goal is to design and test a focused custom hook used by two components. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Cleanup and race prevention; javascript network fetch; Client fetch lifecycle; Dependencies and reactive values. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: A custom hook packages reusable stateful logic and other hooks while each caller receives independent state unless an external store is shared. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Hooks must follow call-order rules, names begin with use, and hidden effects or unstable return identities can surprise consumers. This draft therefore rejects: Calling the same custom hook in two components automatically shares one state instance.

includes: Extract reusable stateful behavior without hiding dependencies or returning unstable accidental APIs.
Design and test a focused custom hook used by two components.
excludes: Hooks must follow call-order rules, names begin with use, and hidden effects or unstable return identities can surprise consumers.

outcomes: Design and test a focused custom hook used by two components.

examples.title: Custom hooks for reusable logic: concrete case
examples.situation: useOnlineStatus owns one subscription pattern and cleans it up for each consuming component.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: useOnlineStatus owns one subscription pattern and cleans it up for each consuming component.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Custom hooks share logic, not state; each call has its own hook state unless both connect to shared external data.
examples.result: The result follows because Custom hooks share logic, not state; each call has its own hook state unless both connect to shared external data.

Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Calling the same custom hook in two components automatically shares one state instance.
